transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Yvette, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yvette: Oh, um, hi. Thanks for having me. Well, I guess, you know, I've been thinking a lot about my aunt lately. It's just, I feel like there's so much I didn't do for her, and it weighs on me. I mean, it's not like I didn't care, but sometimes I wonder if I could have done more, you know? It’s just hard to shake that feeling of, I don’t know, disappointment, I suppose. It’s like I keep going over the same thoughts in my mind, and it just makes me feel... sad, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like your aunt has been on your mind a lot, and you're feeling a mix of sadness and disappointment about what you wish you could have done for her. It’s understandable to have those thoughts replaying in your mind. Can you tell me more about what specific things you feel you could have done differently?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yvette: Well, um, there were just little things, I suppose. Like, I didn’t visit her as often as I should have, and I think about the times I could have called or just checked in more. It’s like, I had all these opportunities, and I didn’t take them. I remember her saying she wanted to spend more time together, and I just... I didn’t make it happen. It’s frustrating, really. I mean, I know life gets busy, but I can’t help but feel like I let her down. It’s just hard to reconcile that, you know? I keep thinking about how I could have made her feel more loved or supported, and it just... it makes me feel heavy inside.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight from those missed opportunities to connect with your aunt. You mentioned feeling frustrated and heavy inside, especially thinking about how you could have made her feel more loved and supported. Can you share more about what those moments mean to you and how they impact your feelings now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yvette: Well, um, those moments, they just feel like, I don’t know, like they’re reminders of what I didn’t do. It’s like, I can picture her face when she talked about wanting to spend time together, and I just... I can’t shake the image of her looking a bit lonely. It’s like I had this chance to be there for her, and I didn’t take it. I think about how, if I had just made the effort, maybe she would have felt less alone. And now, it’s like I’m stuck in this loop of guilt, you know? I keep asking myself why I didn’t prioritize her more. It’s just... it feels like I failed her in a way, and that thought lingers with me. I don’t want to feel this way, but it’s hard to let go.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds really painful to feel like you missed those opportunities to be there for your aunt, and the image of her feeling lonely seems to weigh heavily on you. The guilt you’re experiencing is understandable, especially when you care so much. I’m curious, what do you think it would take for you to begin to let go of some of that guilt?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yvette: Oh, um, that’s a tough question. I guess, I think maybe if I could find a way to honor her memory or something, it might help. Like, if I could do something that reflects how much she meant to me, maybe that would ease some of the guilt. I’ve thought about doing things in her name, like volunteering or just reaching out to others who might feel lonely, you know? It’s like, if I could turn that guilt into something positive, maybe it would feel a little less heavy. But then again, I worry that no matter what I do, it won’t be enough. It’s just... I don’t know, it feels complicated. I want to move forward, but I keep getting pulled back into those thoughts.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re really seeking a way to honor your aunt’s memory and transform that guilt into something meaningful, like volunteering or reaching out to others. That desire to create a positive impact reflects how much you care. At the same time, it seems like there’s a fear that no action you take might ever feel sufficient. Can you tell me more about what that fear feels like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yvette: Oh, um, that fear, it’s like this nagging voice in my head that keeps telling me I won’t be able to do enough, you know? It’s almost like I’m setting myself up for disappointment before I even try. I think about all the things I could do, but then I wonder if they’ll really make a difference or if it’ll just be a way to distract myself from the guilt. It’s like I’m caught between wanting to do something meaningful and feeling like nothing I do will ever measure up to what I wish I could have done when she was here. It’s exhausting, really. I want to feel like I’m honoring her, but this fear just keeps creeping in and making me doubt myself. I guess it’s just hard to find that balance between moving forward and holding onto those memories.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like that fear is really weighing on you, creating a tension between your desire to honor your aunt and the worry that your efforts might not be enough. It’s understandable to feel exhausted by that struggle. I’m curious, when you think about the memories you hold of her, what feelings or thoughts come up for you that might help you navigate this balance?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yvette: Well, um, when I think about the memories I have of her, it’s a mix of warmth and sadness, I suppose. I remember her laughter and how she always had this way of making me feel special, like I was the only one in the room. Those moments bring a smile to my face, but then, right after, I feel that heaviness again, you know? It’s like I can almost hear her voice encouraging me, telling me to let go of the guilt and just remember the good times. But then I think about how I didn’t do enough when she needed me, and it feels like I’m betraying those happy memories by not acting on them. It’s confusing because I want to hold onto the joy she brought into my life, but the guilt just keeps pulling me back. I guess I’m just trying to figure out how to keep her spirit alive without feeling so weighed down by what I didn’t do. It’s a lot to process, really.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re experiencing a deep conflict between the warmth of your happy memories and the heaviness of your guilt. The way you describe her laughter and the feeling of being special really highlights the love you shared. It’s understandable to want to honor her spirit while also grappling with those feelings of regret. What do you think it would look like to hold both the joy of those memories and the guilt at the same time?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yvette: Oh, um, that’s a really interesting question. I think holding both the joy and the guilt would be, well, kind of like acknowledging that it’s okay to feel both things at once, you know? It’s like, I could allow myself to remember the happy times without completely shutting out the sadness. Maybe it would mean finding a way to celebrate her life while also recognizing that I’m human and I make mistakes. I guess it could look like creating a little ritual or something, where I take a moment to reflect on those joyful memories, but also give space for the guilt, too. It’s just, um, I worry that if I let the guilt in, it might overshadow the joy, and I don’t want that. I want to remember her with love, not just with regret. It feels like a delicate balance, and I’m not quite sure how to navigate it yet.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you're beginning to find a way to acknowledge both the joy and the guilt, which is a significant step. Can you share more about what kind of ritual or reflection you might envision that would help you honor her while also embracing those complex emotions?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yvette: Oh, um, I’ve thought about maybe lighting a candle in her memory, you know? Something simple, but meaningful. I could sit quietly and think about all the good times we had together, like those moments when she would make me laugh until my sides hurt. I could also write her a letter, just to express everything I wish I could have said when she was still here. I think that might help me feel connected to her spirit while also acknowledging the guilt I carry. It’s like, I could read the letter aloud while the candle is lit, allowing myself to feel both the joy of our memories and the sadness of what I didn’t do. I just hope that by doing this, I won’t get lost in the guilt, but rather find a way to honor her and keep her memory alive in a way that feels right. It’s just... I guess I’m still figuring out how to make it all fit together without feeling overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yvette: Oh, um, staying grounded, that’s a good point. I think maybe having a few deep breaths before I start could help, you know? I just want to make sure I’m honoring her in a way that feels right, but I guess it’s all about finding that balance, isn’t it? transcript.content: Yvette: Oh, um, that’s a good question. I think maybe setting a little routine for myself could help, you know? Like, before I head out for my walk or reading time, I could take a moment to check in with myself and remind myself of those strategies. Maybe even create a little ritual, like lighting a candle or playing a calming song to signal that it’s time to focus on my intention. I could also set a reminder on my phone, just a gentle nudge to pause and breathe before I dive into the moment. I guess having those little cues could help me remember to use my tools, especially when things start to feel a bit too much. It’s like giving myself permission to take that moment, which feels really important. I just want to make sure I’m not caught off guard and can actually lean into those strategies when I need them.
